section.title: A Gentle Answer
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 15 opens with one of the book's most quoted lines and builds outward from it, gathering sayings on the tongue and the LORD's watching eyes, verses 1 to 8, discipline welcomed or refused, verses 9 to 15, contentment amid little, verses 16 to 20, and timely counsel, justice for the needy, and humility, verses 21 to 33.
section.thesis: A gentle, well timed word can turn away wrath and cheer a crushed spirit, and this whole chapter shows that such gentleness is not weakness but wisdom, rooted in the fear of the LORD, who sees every heart and finally exalts the humble.
section.frameNote: Five thematic clusters: the tongue and the watching eyes of the LORD, verses 1 to 8; discipline welcomed or refused, verses 9 to 15; contentment with little over turmoil with much, verses 16 to 20; timely counsel and justice for the needy, verses 21 to 27; and righteous speech, humility, and the fear of the LORD, verses 28 to 33.

units.label: The tongue and the watching eyes of the LORD
units.note: words that heal, worship that pleases

verses.text: A {{rakh|gentle}} answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger.
The tongue of the wise commends knowledge, but the mouth of the fool spouts folly.
The {{ayin|eyes}} of the LORD are in every place, observing the evil and the good.
A soothing tongue is a tree of life, but a perverse tongue crushes the spirit.
A fool rejects his father's discipline, but whoever heeds correction is prudent.
The house of the righteous has great treasure, but the income of the wicked is trouble.
The lips of the wise spread knowledge, but not so the hearts of fools.
The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable to the LORD, but the prayer of the upright is His delight.

units.label: Discipline welcomed or refused
units.note: a joyful or a crushed spirit

verses.text: The LORD detests the way of the wicked, but He loves those who pursue righteousness.
Discipline is harsh for him who leaves the path; he who hates correction will die.
Sheol and Abaddon lie open before the LORD, how much more the hearts of men!
A mocker does not love to be reproved, nor will he consult the wise.
A {{simchah|joyful}} heart makes a cheerful countenance, but sorrow of the heart crushes the spirit.
A discerning heart seeks knowledge, but the mouth of a fool feeds on folly.
All the days of the oppressed are bad, but a cheerful heart has a continual feast.
Better a little with the fear of the LORD than great treasure with turmoil.
Better a dish of vegetables where there is love than a fattened ox with hatred.
A hot-tempered man stirs up strife, but he who is slow to anger calms dispute.
The way of the slacker is like a hedge of thorns, but the path of the upright is a highway.
A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish man despises his mother.

units.label: Timely counsel and justice for the needy

verses.text: Folly is joy to one who lacks judgment, but a man of understanding walks a straight path.
Plans fail for lack of counsel, but with many advisers they succeed.
A man takes joy in a fitting reply, and how good is a timely word!
The path of life leads upward for the wise, that he may avoid going down to Sheol.
The LORD tears down the house of the proud, but He protects the boundaries of the widow.
The LORD detests the thoughts of the wicked, but the words of the pure are pleasant to Him.
He who is greedy for unjust gain brings trouble on his household, but he who hates bribes will live.

units.label: Righteous speech, humility, and the fear of the LORD

verses.text: The heart of the righteous ponders how to answer, but the mouth of the wicked blurts out evil.
The LORD is far from the wicked, but He hears the prayer of the righteous.
The light of the eyes cheers the heart, and good news nourishes the bones.
He who listens to life-giving reproof will dwell among the wise.
He who ignores discipline despises himself, but whoever heeds correction gains understanding.
The fear of the LORD is the instruction of wisdom, and {{anavah|humility}} comes before honor.

rakh.gloss: Gentle, soft, or tender, describing the answer that turns away wrath in the chapter's opening line, verse 1. The word pictures strength under control, a deliberate softness chosen in response to provocation rather than weakness or passivity.

ayin.gloss: Eyes, used in verse 3 of the LORD's watching presence in every place, observing both evil and good. This constant divine attention frames the whole chapter's confidence that nothing, not even hidden hearts, verse 11, ultimately escapes God's notice.

simchah.gloss: Joy or gladness, contrasted throughout this chapter with crushed or sorrowful spirits, verses 13, 15, 30. The chapter treats a joyful heart not as mere emotional luck but as something closely tied to wisdom, contentment, and good relationships.

anavah.gloss: Humility, named in the chapter's closing verse as what comes before honor, verse 33, forming a fitting final word to a chapter that has repeatedly shown gentleness, contentment, and welcomed correction to be marks of true wisdom, not weakness.

basic.context: This chapter opens with one of the most familiar lines in all of Proverbs, a gentle answer turns away wrath, and builds outward from there. It moves through sayings on the tongue and God's all seeing eyes, welcomed or rejected discipline, contentment amid little, timely counsel, and God's justice for the proud and the needy, closing with humility as the doorway to honor.
basic.hermeneutics: The opening proverb sets the tone for reading the whole chapter, gentleness, welcomed correction, and contentment are not signs of a weak or passive life but of genuine wisdom, while harshness, mockery, and greed, however forceful they feel, are folly. As elsewhere, these are general, trustworthy patterns of how life under God's moral order tends to unfold, memorable and true, though not mechanical guarantees removing every exception.
basic.originalLanguages: Rakh, gentle, verse 1, names a deliberate, chosen softness, not weakness, that can defuse anger a harsh word would only inflame. Ayin, the eyes of the LORD, verse 3, pictures God's attention as covering every place, evil and good alike. Simchah, joy, recurs across the chapter as closely tied to wisdom and contentment, not mere circumstance. And anavah, humility, closes the chapter as the very doorway to honor, verse 33.
basic.typology: The chapter's opening gentle answer, verse 1, anticipates Christ's own teaching on turning the other cheek and returning blessing for insult, Matthew 5:39, and His own example of gentle, humble bearing, described as gentle and humble in heart, Matthew 11:29.
basic.christ: This chapter's opening wisdom, a gentle answer turns away wrath, finds its perfect embodiment in Christ, who taught His followers to turn the other cheek, Matthew 5:39, and who described Himself as gentle and humble in heart, Matthew 11:29, even while facing false accusation and violence with no retaliation, 1 Peter 2:23. And the chapter's closing claim, humility comes before honor, verse 33, is the very pattern of the gospel itself, Christ humbled Himself even to death on a cross, therefore God highly exalted Him, Philippians 2:8-9, the pattern every believer is called to follow.
basic.god: God's eyes are in every place, verse 3, missing nothing, evil or good, and even Sheol and Abaddon lie open before Him, verse 11, how much more the hearts of the living. He is far from the wicked yet attentive to the prayer of the righteous, verse 29, and He actively tears down the proud while protecting the vulnerable widow, verse 25, showing His justice reaches both the mighty and the defenseless.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 5:39 has Christ teach turning the other cheek, echoing the gentle answer of verse 1. Matthew 11:29 describes Christ as gentle and humble in heart. Philippians 2:8-9 traces the pattern of humility before exaltation, echoing verse 33. James 4:10 and 1 Peter 5:6 both call believers to humble themselves so God may exalt them in due time.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's confidence that gentleness, contentment, and humility are the true marks of wisdom, while harshness and pride lead only to ruin, belongs to Scripture's larger story of a God who sees everything and finally exalts the humble. That story reaches its fullest expression in Christ, whose gentle answer to violence and whose humility before His own exaltation are the pattern this whole chapter was already pointing toward.

context.literary: A continuing sequence of independent proverbs, distinguished by an unusually high concentration of sayings on the tongue and on God's watching presence, verses 1 to 8, before broadening into discipline, contentment, counsel, and humility, culminating in one of the collection's most quoted closing lines on humility before honor, verse 33.
context.historical: The reference to protecting the boundaries of the widow, verse 25, reflects ancient Israel's concern for the most vulnerable members of society, whose land boundaries could easily be encroached upon without a husband to defend them, a concern echoed throughout the Law and the Prophets.
context.canonical: Verse 33's closing pairing, the fear of the LORD and humility before honor, anticipates the consistent New Testament pattern of humility preceding exaltation, most fully realized in the humiliation and exaltation of Christ Himself, Philippians 2:5-11, and echoed in James 4:10 and 1 Peter 5:6.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend gentleness, contentment, welcomed correction, and humility as the true marks of wisdom throughout ordinary conflict, family life, and worship, grounding all of it in confidence that the LORD's eyes see everything and He finally exalts the humble and vindicates the needy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Direct commands and evaluations, a gentle answer, better a little with the fear of the LORD, are prescriptive wisdom for how to live. Broader statements, the LORD tears down the house of the proud, verse 25, describe the normal, trustworthy shape of God's moral government of history rather than a fixed timetable for every individual case; the same chapter's confidence in verse 3, that God's eyes are everywhere, is the deeper ground that makes these proverbial patterns trustworthy even when their outworking is not always immediately visible.

debates.question: Does verse 1's gentle answer mean confrontation or firm rebuke is never appropriate?

views.label: A general wisdom principle about tone, not a rule against ever confronting sin
views.case: Proverbs elsewhere commends direct rebuke of fools and even Christ Himself spoke very sharply to the proud and hypocritical, Matthew 23, so verse 1 is best read as commending a wise, de escalating tone in ordinary conflict, not forbidding all firmness.

views.label: A strong general preference for gentleness as the default posture
views.case: The verse states a confident, near universal pattern, gentle answers turn away wrath, harsh words stir it up, suggesting gentleness should be the default instinct even when correction is necessary, reserving sharper words for exceptional cases.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that gentleness is the wise default in ordinary relational conflict, while Scripture elsewhere shows that firm, even sharp, rebuke has its place against hardened sin or hypocrisy; the wisdom lies in discerning which situation calls for which response, not in applying either gentleness or firmness mechanically in every case.

study.originalLanguages: Rakh, gentle, verse 1, names a chosen, deliberate softness with real power to defuse conflict. Ayin, the eyes of the LORD, verse 3, pictures divine attention reaching every place, a confidence deepened by verse 11's claim that even Sheol and Abaddon lie open before Him. Simchah, joy, threads through the chapter as a mark of wisdom and contentment rather than mere luck of circumstance. And anavah, humility, closes the chapter as the very doorway to honor, verse 33, a principle the New Testament will develop fully in the pattern of Christ's own humiliation and exaltation.

typology.type: A gentle answer that turns away wrath, Proverbs 15:1
typology.fulfillment: Christ's teaching to turn the other cheek and His own gentle, humble bearing under false accusation, Matthew 5:39, 11:29, 1 Peter 2:23.
Christ, who humbled Himself to the point of death, therefore God highly exalted Him, Philippians 2:8-9.

study.christ: The chapter's opening wisdom, a gentle answer turns away wrath, verse 1, is embodied perfectly in Christ, who taught His followers to turn the other cheek, Matthew 5:39, described Himself as gentle and humble in heart, Matthew 11:29, and, when reviled, did not revile in return, 1 Peter 2:23. And the chapter's closing principle, humility comes before honor, verse 33, is the very shape of the gospel, Christ humbled Himself, becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross, therefore God highly exalted Him, Philippians 2:8-9, the pattern this whole chapter's wisdom was quietly anticipating all along.
study.god: God's eyes reach every place, missing nothing, evil or good, verse 3, and His knowledge penetrates even Sheol and Abaddon, how much more the hearts of the living, verse 11. He actively opposes the proud while protecting the vulnerable widow, verse 25, and He is far from the wicked yet near to the prayer of the righteous, verse 29, a God whose justice is neither blind nor indifferent to how people actually live.

crossReferences.note: Turn the other cheek, echoing the gentle, wrath deflecting answer of verse 1.
Christ describes Himself as gentle and humble in heart, embodying this chapter's ideal.
Christ humbled Himself, therefore God exalted Him, the pattern behind verse 33's humility before honor.
Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand, that He may exalt you in due time, echoing verse 33.

study.oneStory: This chapter's confidence that gentleness, contentment, and humility mark true wisdom, while harshness and pride lead to ruin, belongs to Scripture's ongoing story of a God who sees all things and exalts the humble in His own time. That story reaches its fullest expression in Christ, whose gentle response to violence and whose humbling unto death, followed by exaltation, are the pattern this entire chapter had already begun to trace.

quiz.q: What does verse 1 say about the power of a gentle answer?

options.text: It turns away wrath, while a harsh word stirs up anger.
options.note: This is the exact statement and one of the book's most quoted lines.

options.text: It has no real effect on how conflict unfolds.
options.note: The verse claims a real, significant effect on defusing wrath.

options.text: It works only in formal, official settings.
options.note: The proverb applies generally to any conflict, not a restricted formal setting.

options.text: It means avoiding all confrontation, even over serious sin.
options.note: The verse commends a wise tone in conflict; it does not by itself forbid ever addressing serious wrongdoing elsewhere in Scripture.

quiz.q: What does verse 3 say about God's awareness of human affairs?

options.text: His eyes are in every place, observing both evil and good.
options.note: This is the exact claim of the verse.

options.text: He is unaware of hidden or private matters.
options.note: Verse 11 reinforces the opposite, even Sheol and Abaddon lie open before Him.

options.text: He observes only public, visible actions.
options.note: The chapter's broader claim, including verse 11, extends His awareness to hidden hearts, not just visible actions.

options.text: He is watching only the wicked, not the righteous.
options.note: The verse specifically says He observes both evil and good, not one group exclusively.

quiz.q: According to verses 16 to 17, what is better than wealth accompanied by turmoil or hatred?

options.text: A little with the fear of the LORD, or a simple meal shared in love.
options.note: Both verses draw exactly this contrast between modest, peaceful contentment and great wealth marred by conflict.

options.text: Great wealth, regardless of relational cost.
options.note: The verses explicitly favor peace and the fear of the LORD over wealth accompanied by turmoil or hatred.

options.text: Complete poverty is always preferable to any wealth.
options.note: The contrast is with wealth accompanied by turmoil or hatred specifically, not wealth as such.

options.text: Wealth and love are entirely unrelated concerns in this passage.
options.note: The verses directly compare and relate the two, rather than treating them as unrelated.

quiz.q: What does verse 33 say comes before honor?

options.text: Humility.
options.note: This is the exact closing statement of the chapter.

options.text: Wealth.
options.note: The verse names humility, not wealth, as preceding honor.

options.text: Political power.
options.note: The verse's concern is moral and spiritual, not political standing.

options.text: Public recognition sought directly.
options.note: The verse's point is nearly the opposite, humility, not the direct pursuit of recognition, precedes true honor.

quiz.q: How does the chapter's teaching on humility before honor connect to Christ?

options.text: Christ humbled Himself to the point of death, and therefore God highly exalted Him, embodying this exact pattern.
options.note: Philippians 2:8-9 describes precisely this sequence in Christ's own life.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Philippians 2 draws directly on this humility before exaltation pattern in describing Christ.

options.text: It teaches that Christ sought honor directly rather than through humility.
options.note: The New Testament stresses the opposite, Christ's exaltation followed His voluntary humbling, not a direct pursuit of honor.

options.text: It applies only to Old Testament kings, not to Christ.
options.note: The New Testament applies this very principle directly and centrally to Christ Himself, not only to ancient kings.

questions: Verse 1 says a gentle answer turns away wrath. Think of your last real conflict. Did your words tend to de escalate or inflame it?
Verse 3 reminds us that God's eyes are in every place. How does remembering that you are always seen by God change how you act when no one else is watching?

Verses 16 to 17 say a little with the fear of the LORD, or a simple meal with love, is better than wealth with turmoil or hatred. Are you currently sacrificing peace or love for the sake of gaining more?
Verse 22 says plans fail for lack of counsel but succeed with many advisers. What decision are you facing right now that you have not yet sought wise counsel about?
Verse 33 says humility comes before honor, the very pattern of Christ's own humbling and exaltation. Where is pride keeping you from the kind of humility that leads to true honor in God's eyes?

translationNotes.note: Sheol and Abaddon can also be rendered Death and Destruction, both terms for the realm of the dead, used together for poetic emphasis on how thoroughly nothing is hidden from the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 15:1-33. Two em dashes present in the source, at verses 11 and 23, were rewritten using commas to comply with the no dash rule; the wording is otherwise unchanged and complete.
